title: Harmonization

content.question: Where is internal conflict draining you that harmonization would resolve?
sop: List the recurring points of friction between people, teams, or parts of your own schedule.
For each, assign one person (or yourself, for personal conflicts) as the harmonizer responsible for resolving it.
Set a short daily or weekly check-in specifically to catch friction before it compounds.
Resolve one conflict fully this week instead of letting it simmer, count it as resolved only when both sides agree it's settled (see calculation, page 5).
Track how many resolutions actually stick after 30 days, not just how many conversations happened.
questions: Where is internal conflict draining you that harmonization would resolve?
Which two people, teams, or parts of your own schedule are consistently working against each other instead of in sync?
What recurring friction have you been treating as 'just how it is' instead of something to actually resolve?
content.equation_example: Count of friction points actually resolved (not just discussed) in a given period. 3 conflicts fully resolved this month gives a score of 3. Higher and rising means friction is being cleared faster than it's created; flat or zero means conflicts are accumulating unresolved.
content.closing_statement: Unresolved friction compounds quietly until it doesn't.

caption:
Principle Nº 202: Harmonization

Smooth frictions between parts.

Ask yourself: Where is internal conflict draining you that harmonization would resolve?
